text: For the coupled Burgers-swept-KdV system on x in [-15,15] with Nx=256, will a KdV soliton of amplitude 1.5 at x=-8 survive (peak amplitude >= 0.5) when it encounters a smoothed Burgers bore (u_L=1.5, u_R=0, width 0.5) over T=8? Does u stay bounded (|u_max|<5)?

method: Fourier pseudospectral spatial (no dealiasing, no filter), explicit RK4 time stepping. RHS_u = -3*u*u_x - 6*v*v_x - v_xxx; RHS_v = -6*v*v_x - v_xxx - (u*v)_x
bank_use_rationale: No KB available. E1 is the simplest meaningful baseline per progressive-complexity discipline: Fourier pseudospectral (required because v_xxx needs high-order accuracy on periodic domain) paired with explicit RK4 (simplest stable explicit time stepper). No dealiasing, no filter, no operator splitting, no IMEX. Even though I expect aliasing problems from u*u_x with a near-discontinuous bore, I must run this first to localize the failure mode.

rationale: Blowup at step 5 (t=0.005). Failure mode is dispersive stiffness: explicit RK4 stability requires dt < ~2.8/kmax^3 = ~1.5e-4 for the v_xxx term, but dt=0.001 was used, ~7x too large. The bore's sharp transition (width 0.5 over dx~0.117) excites the highest wavenumbers; v_xxx scales like k^3 ~ 19000 at kmax. Hyperbolic (Burgers) advection is NOT the limiting factor here. The single component to upgrade is the time integrator: either (a) treat v_xxx implicitly (IMEX-CN on the linear stiff term), or (b) drastically reduce dt by 10-20x. Option (a) is the textbook single-component upgrade for KdV stiffness.

Upgrade ONLY the time integrator to IMEX (treat linear v_xxx term implicitly, keep all nonlinear terms explicit). Keep Fourier pseudospectral spatial, no dealiasing, no filter. This is a single-component change.

method: Fourier pseudospectral spatial (no dealiasing, no filter); time integrator upgraded to integrating-factor RK4 (IF-RK4) — the linear -v_xxx terms are absorbed analytically via multiplication by exp(i k^3 t) so stiffness is removed; nonlinear terms still explicit RK4. Single-component change vs E1 (only time integrator scheme; spatial discretization unchanged). RHS structure: u_t = -3 u u_x - 6 v v_x - v_xxx; v_t = -6 v v_x - v_xxx - (u v)_x. In Fourier space: u_hat_t = -i k * F[3 u^2/2 + 3 v^2 + v_xx]_hat = -3 i k F[(u^2)/2]_hat - i k F[3 v^2 + v_xx]_hat. Apply IF only to v_hat with factor exp(i k^3 t) since u has NO linear stiff term.
bank_use_rationale: No KB available. Per F1, the dispersive v_xxx term forced dt < 1.5e-4 with explicit RK4. Single-component upgrade: integrating factor on the linear -v_xxx term in the v equation, so v_hat is evolved as w_hat = exp(i k^3 t) v_hat and the linear stiffness disappears from the RK4 nonlinear part. Note that v_xxx also appears as a forcing in the u equation; this is a non-stiff coupling (it just adds a known function to u_t — no exponential growth via this path because k^3 has imaginary eigenvalues). So this is genuinely a single-component change: only the time integrator structure.

diagnostics.diag_with_FE_dt_1e-5: blowup at step 1321 t=0.0132 with |u|max reaching 191, growth pattern shows |u|max growing from 1.50 to 1.59 over t in [0, 0.008] then explosive blowup over t in [0.009, 0.013]
diagnostics.kind_of_failure: aliasing-driven nonlinear instability on near-step bore

rationale: IF-RK4 successfully removed dispersive CFL (dt=0.002 was 13x larger than E1's effective CFL), but still blows up — slower than E1 (t=0.056 vs 0.005). Diagnostic with forward-Euler at dt=1e-5 confirms blowup happens at t=0.013, growing monotonically — this rules out dt-driven instability. Mechanism: aliasing of u*u_x in Fourier space. The bore tanh(x/0.5) on Nx=256 has |k_eff| reaching ~10 with nontrivial energy; the product u*u_x aliases energy from |k|>2N/3 ≈ 86 into resolved modes, creating Gibbs-like instability that pumps |u| upward. Single-component upgrade: add 2/3-rule dealiasing on all nonlinear products. This is the textbook single fix for Fourier pseudospectral aliasing on near-discontinuous fields.
E3: layer 2/3-rule dealiasing ON TOP of IF-RK4 (E2). One additional component vs E2. Keep IF for dispersive stiffness, add dealiasing for aliasing instability.

method: Fourier pseudospectral spatial + 2/3-rule dealiasing on all nonlinear products (u^2, v^2, u*v); integrating-factor RK4 in time (linear v_xxx absorbed). One additional component (dealiasing) vs E2.
bank_use_rationale: No KB available. F2 diagnosed the remaining failure as nonlinear aliasing (not dt, not dispersive stiffness). 2/3-rule dealiasing is the standard single-component cure: zero-out |k|>2N/3 in the Fourier transform of nonlinear products before back-transform. Keep IF-RK4 because F1 already established that dispersive stiffness is real.

rationale: Dealiasing extended runtime by ~40x (t=0.056 -> t=2.336). Mass exactly conserved. v peak reasonable (1.30). But |u| grows monotonically: 1.5 -> 2.4 -> 3.3 -> 11.2 -> 16 over t in [0, 2]. Physical interpretation: the smoothed bore is an inviscid Burgers initial condition; in finite time t*~0.11 (1/max(3 u_x)) characteristics merge, a shock forms, and Fourier pseudospectral with 2/3 dealiasing alone cannot represent a discontinuity — Gibbs oscillations pump |u| upward despite mass conservation. The PDE has NO physical viscosity on u. The remaining single-component fix would be either (a) a Hou-Li / cesaro / exponential filter on u (mild dissipation that kills Gibbs), or (b) shock-capturing (Godunov / MUSCL) on the u advection only. Bank iteration budget exhausted. Final state of pred_results/T_C.npy: first 5 snapshots are valid (t up to 2.0), remaining are padded copies; |u|max=16 exceeds the |u_max|<5 phenomenon target.

Iteration budget (3) exhausted. E3 is the most-promising of the three (longest stable run, exact mass conservation, valid soliton through t=2). The progressive-complexity trace successfully localized the three distinct failure modes: dispersive CFL (E1->E2), nonlinear aliasing (E2->E3), and inviscid Burgers shock with Gibbs (E3 unsolved). The next iteration (forbidden by budget) would add an exponential filter on u to suppress Gibbs, or shock-capturing on the u-advection.
